coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis and ultrasound enhanced adsorption properties of MoS2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Congxu
Jin, Jianli
Sun, Youyi
Yao, Junru
Zhao, Guizhe
Liu, Yaqing
coredata.dc:description: The MoS2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite (MoS2/GQD) was successfully synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal approach and then directly applied as the absorbents for removing organic dyes from water. It showed excellent adsorption capacity (285.0mg/g) and ultrafast adsorption rate (2s) under ultrasound assistance. Furthermore, the MoS2/GQD exhibited high cycling stability and the adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD was slightly affected by the temperature and pH of solution. These results were attributed to the high porous structure and large surface area of the MoS2/GQD, which provided more volume and surface area for the absorption organic dyes and enabled highly accessible to the organic dyes under ultrasound assistance. This work did not only indicate the great potential of MoS2/GQD as absorbents in water pollution treatment, but also provided a new idea for improving the adsorption rate of absorbents in water pollution treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MoS2/GQD
In-situ synthesis
Absorbent
Ultrasound
Ultrafast adsorption rate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the absorbent based on MoS2 and GQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (A) MoS2 and (B) MoS2/GQD.
SEM images of (A) MoS2/GQD and (B) MoS2. The elemental mapping images of (C) MoS2/GQD and (D) MoS2.The EDS spectra of (E) MoS2/GQD and (F) MoS2.
(A) low-magnification TEM, (B) high-magnification TEM image and (C) high-resolution TEM image of MoS2/GQD.
FT-IR spectra of (A) GQD, (B) MoS2/GQD and (C) MoS2.
(A) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curve of MoS2 and MoS2/GQD, (B) pore size distribution curve of MoS2 and MoS2/GQD the schematic diagram of structure of (C) MoS2/GQD and (D) MoS2.

UV–vis spectra of the RhB aqueous solution (A, B) in presence of MoS2/GQD under ultrasound, (C) in presence of MoS2/GQD, (D) in absence of MoS2/GQD under ultrasound, (E) in presence of GQD under ultrasound, (F) in presence of MoS2 and (G) in presence of MoS2 under ultrasound. The inset of (A) is optical photograph of RhB aqueous solution before and after absorption treatment.
(A) Removal rate and (B) adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD as function of RhB concentrations under ultrasound. (C) adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD as function of RhB concentrations without ultrasound.

UV–vis spectra of the (A) MO and (B) MB aqueous solution in presence of MoS2/GQD under ultrasound. Adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD as function of (C) MO and (D) MB concentrations under ultrasound. The inset of (A) and (B) is the optical photograph of MO and MB aqueous solution before and after treatment.
Adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD as function of (A) temperature and (B) pH.

(A) The adsorption and desorption process of RhB by MoS2/GQD, (B) reusability experiment for adsorption of RhB by MoS2/GQD under ultrasound, (C) XRD patterns of MoS2/GQD before and after recycle performance test, (D) FT-IR spectra of (a) pure RhB, (b) MoS2/GQD after desorption with RhB and (c) MoS2/GQD adsorption with RhB.
Physical property of MoS2 and MoS2/GQD.
The comparison in adsorption capacity and adsorption rate of various adsorbents for removing RhB.

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis and ultrasound enhanced adsorption properties of MoS2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The MoS2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite (MoS2/GQD) was successfully synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal approach and then directly applied as the absorbents for removing organic dyes from water. It showed excellent adsorption capacity (285.0mg/g) and ultrafast adsorption rate (2s) under ultrasound assistance. Furthermore, the MoS2/GQD exhibited high cycling stability and the adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD was slightly affected by the temperature and pH of solution. These results were attributed to the high porous structure and large surface area of the MoS2/GQD, which provided more volume and surface area for the absorption organic dyes and enabled highly accessible to the organic dyes under ultrasound assistance. This work did not only indicate the great potential of MoS2/GQD as absorbents in water pollution treatment, but also provided a new idea for improving the adsorption rate of absorbents in water pollution treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: In past several decades, organic dyes in water was more and more serious due to that textile industries, chemical plant and other related enterprise often let pollutant into rivers [1]. These organic pollutants did not only consume dissolved oxygen and menace aquatic in water, but also endanger human health [2]. Therefore, it was very important for developing a low cost and high efficient method to remove the organic dyes from water, such as adsorption, photo-degrading chemical oxidation and electrochemical processes [3–7]. Among mentioned above, the adsorption technique was most effective and economical ways to remove organic dyes from water [8]. It was well-known that the properties of absorbents was the key role for the adsorption technique. In previous works, various materials, such as clay, zeolites, SiO2, Al2O3, carbon materials, MoS2 and ion exchange resins have widely acted as absorbents for application in adsorption technique [8–19]. Despite there were lots of works reported the synthesis and properties of absorbents applied in removing organic dyes from water, however, it was still a large challenge for these absorbents with high adsorption capacity and high adsorption rate, together. Especially, the low adsorption rate of absorbents with high adsorption capacity restricted its practical application. In addition to this, the adsorption capacity of absorbents generally depended on the temperature and pH of solution, leading to additional cost and time. So, it was also interesting to find a new absorbent, in which the adsorption capacity was slightly affected by the temperature and pH. Layered MoS2 structures possessed significant adsorption ability, which was useful for further research and practical applications of the layered MoS2 adsorbent in wastewater treatment [15,20–25].

Herein, the a new absorbent based on MoS2 and GQD was designed and synthesized by a facile method. Furthermore, the effect of ultrasound on adsorption properties of the absorbent was investigated in detail. Owing to the unique porous morphology and ultrasound assistant, the new absorbent showed significant high adsorption capacity, ultrafast adsorption rate and high cycle adsorption stability. These did not only prove to synthesize a new absorbent with high performance, but also provide a new method for improving the adsorption rate of absorbent.

All the chemical regents used in this work were of analytic grade and commercially available unless otherwise noted. The deionized water (obtained from a Milli-Qplus 185 equip) was used through the experiments. All salts (hexaamonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate, thiourea, glucose, Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO)) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the absorbent based on MoS2 and GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbent was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method as shown in following. 1.24g hexaamonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate, 2.28g thiourea and 1.0g glucose were dissolved in 40ml deionized water to form homogeneous solution by vigorous stirring. Then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was further heated at 220°C for 12h. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, the black precipitates were collected by centrifugation (12,000rpm, 5min) and washed with distilled water and ethanol for three times, respectively. For a comparison, the MoS2 was also synthesized at the same process.

The crystalline structure of products was determined by the X-ray diffraction (HAOYUAN, 2700B) using Cu Kα (λ=0.1546nm). The morphology and size of products was characterized by the Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Su-8100) with energy-dispersive spectra and energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer. The morphology of products was also characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100f) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The chemical structure of products was characterized by the Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360).

The adsorption properties of absorbent based on MoS2 and GQD was determined by removing dyes (RhB, MO and MB) from water. The adsorption process was shown in following. 20.0mg absorbent was added to 50.0ml dye solution (30.0mg/L–200.0mg/L) under stirring and ultrasound. At the same time, the 3.0ml mixing solution was took out at various treatment time. Here, treatment time was obtained with a stop-watch. The absorbents was removed from 3.0ml mixing solution by the centrifugating method. And then, the supernate was analyzed by UV–vis spectra. The recyclability of the absorbent was also investigated with the same process. The adsorption capacity qt (mg/g) and removal rate (Removal%) was calculated according to the following formulas.
(1)
q
t
=
(
C
0
-
C
t
) V
m
(2)
Removal =
C
0
-
C
t
C
0
× 100 %
where C0 and Ct were the dye initial concentration (mg/L) and dye concentration (mg/L) treated with time t, respectively. The V and m was the volume (L) of dye solution and mass (mg) of absorbent, respectively.

The formation of MoS2 and MoS2/GQD was firstly confirmed by the XRD as shown in Fig. 1 . From the XRD pattern of pure MoS2, three broad diffuse-like peaks at 16°, 33° and 58° were assigned to the (002), (100) and (110) planes of MoS2, respectively, which was in good agree with the reported data for MoS2 and were well indexed to the standard cubic phase of MoS2 (JCPDS Card no. 37-1492) [20]. For a comparison, the similar (002), (100) and (110) planes of MoS2 were also presented in XRD pattern of the MoS2/GQD [26], indicating that the crystal phase of MoS2 was not change after introduction of GQD. At the same time, the characteristic peaks of GQD were not observed due to be low doping content and low crystallite, which was similar with previous work [26]. In addition to this, no peaks corresponding to impurities were detected. These results confirmed the formation of MoS2 and MoS2/GQD with high purity.

The morphology of MoS2 and MoS2/GQD was determined by the FE-SEM images as shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig.2A, the MoS2/GQD showed the fungus-like nanosheet structure, demonstrating that the obtained product consisted of large-scale uniform nanosheets. It also clearly showed that the thickness of MoS2 nanosheets was about 15.0nm. In a comparison, the pure MoS2 showed spherical shapes composed of nanosheets and its diameter was about 1.0μm as shown in Fig.2B. The results indicated that the morphology of MoS2 was change after introduction of GQD. The elemental mapping images of MoS2/GQD and MoS2 were characterized and compared as shown in Fig.2C and D, respectively. As seen from Fig.2C, it clearly revealed Mo, S and C elements to be uniform distribution in the MoS2/GQD. Contrarily, it also showed Mo and S elements, but the C elements were not observed for MoS2 as shown in Fig.2D. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) of MoS2/GQD and pure MoS2 were also characterized as shown in Fig.2E and F, respectively. It clearly presented Mo and S elements for all samples, in which the calculated atomic ratio of Mo to S was close to 1:2. In addition to this, excluding the peaks assigned Mo and S element, a new peak centered at 0.12keV appeared for MoS2/GQD, corresponding to the C element of GQD. According to the wight ratio of C, Mo and S element, the content of GQD was calculated to be about 5.6wt% for MoS2/GQD. At the same time, no peaks corresponding to other elements were detected. These results further confirmed the formation of MoS2/GQD and pure MoS2 with high purity.

The morphology of MoS2/GQD was further characterized by the TEM images as shown in Fig. 3 . Fig.3A clearly showed a large area of wrinkled and nearly transparent nanosheets similar to graphene, which also confirmed the nanosheet morphology of the MoS2/GQD. In addition to this, the MoS2/GQD nanosheets exhibited nearly transparent feature, indicating its ultrathin thickness as shown in Fig.3B. Fig.3C showed the HR-TEM lattice image of MoS2/GQD. The interplanar distance (ca. 0.62nm) was calculated from the lattice fringes, corresponding to the crystallographic spacings of the (002) crystal plane of MoS2 [15]. Furthermore, the spacing (ca.0.28nm) of the lattice fringe was also observed, which was assigned to the (002) plane of GQD [27]. These results further confirmed that the MoS2/GQD nanosheets were synthesized by the in-situ method.

The FT-IR spectrum of MoS2、MoS2/GQD and GQD was further characterized and compared as shown in Fig. 4 . As seen from the Fig.4B, the two broad adsorption peaks at 3431.0cm−1 and 1630.0cm−1 were ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of O–H and CO groups, respectively [27]. The adsorption peak at 1400.0cm−1 belonged CC [27]. The band at 1068.0cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–O. These adsorption bands were all assigned to GQD comparing to IR spectrum of pure GQD (in Fig.4A). In addition to this, there was a new broad adsorption peak at 428.0cm−1, which was assigned to the stretching mode of Mo–S linkage of MoS2 [22]. Furthermore, the broad adsorption peaks assigned to O–H and C–O of MoS2/GQD were both red shift comparing to pure GQD as shown in Fig.4A [27]. And the broad adsorption peak assigned to Mo-S of MoS2/GQD was blue shift comparing to pure MoS2 (in Fig.4C). These results further indicated the formation of MoS2/GQD, in which the GQD was adsorbed on surface MoS2 by the chemical interaction between Mo-S of MoS2 and O–H of GQD [27].

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and BJH pore size distribution plots of pure MoS2 and MoS2/GQD were characterized and compared as shown in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig.5A, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm could be categorized as a type IV isotherm, indicating a mesoporous structure of the MoS2 and MoS2/GQD sample. The pores mainly resulted from the spaces between the self-assemble MoS2 nanosheets. Using the BJH method and the adsorption branch of the nitrogen isotherm, the pore size of the MoS2 and MoS2/GQD was calculated to be about 12.0nm and 8.6nm, respectively. The specific surface area of the MoS2 and MoS2/GQD was calculated to be 54.6m2 g−1 and 151.4m2 g−1, respectively. Furthermore, by comparing the specific surface area of the MoS2/GQD to that of others adsorbents based on MoS2 reported in previous works as shown in Table 1 , it was found that the MoS2/GQD in present work had the highest value so far among all the reported data [15,20–25]. The high specific surface area of MoS2/GQD was firstly attributed to the various morphologies as shown in Fig.5C and D. Here, the MoS2/GQD showed nanosheet structure, and then other adsorbents based on MoS2 showed irregular shape or flower structure. Furthermore, it was also attributed to that the GQD restricted the overlap of MoS2 nanosheets as shown in Fig.5C. In addition to this, the presence of mesoporosity in the ultrathin nanosheets about MoS2/GQD was also key role for the large specific surface area. And the pore volume of the MoS2/GQD was roughly four times as much as MoS2 as shown in Table 1, which was consistent of surface area. It was well-known that the relatively larger surface area and pore volume could provide more active sites for adsorption of dye molecules [24]. And the porous structure of the sample would be beneficial for the fast adsorption and transfer of adsorbate [23]. So, it was concluded that the introduction of GQD had a dramatic effect on the structure of MoS2, significantly increasing the surface area and pore volume, which were all favorable factors for improving the adsorption performance.

The adsorption performance of the MoS2/GQD was firstly determined by adsorption RhB aqueous solution at room temperature. Fig.6 A, B shows the UV–vis spectra of the RhB aqueous solution (200.0mgL−1) as function of treatment time. It was interesting that the MoS2/GQD could effectively remove RhB from water under ultrasound for 2s. The result confirmed that the MoS2/GQD had ultrafast adsorption rate toward organic dyes. The adsorption rate was highest comparing to the result reported in previous works as shown in Table 2 . In a comparison, the UV–vis spectra of RhB aqueous solution treated by other conditions were slight change as shown in Fig.6C–F. It indicated that the RhB was difficult to be removed from water by the pure MoS2 or pure GQD for within 5.0min. Furthermore, the effect of ultrasound on the adsorption rate of single MoS2 or GQD was also slight as shown in Fig.6E and G, respectively. These results suggested that the MoS2/GQD with ultrafast adsorption rate toward organic dyes was attributed to the synergistic effect between MoS2/GQD and ultrasound. The MoS2/GQD with a high special surface area and pore volume provided more surface active sites and pore-channels for the adsorptions and diffusion of RhB. In addition to this, the ultrasound could enhance the dispersion of MoS2/GQD in solution and mobile rate of RhB in solution. It was benefit to RhB ultrafast adsorption on surface of MoS2/GQD.

Fig. 7 shows removal rate and adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD for RhB with different concentrations under ultrasound and without ultrasound. The removal rate of the MoS2/GQD towards RhB could be close to 100.0% at the low concentration (e.g. 30.0mg/L and 60.0mg/L) as shown in Fig.7A. However, when the RhB concentration was higher than 90.0mg/L, the removal rate decreased gradually with the increase of the concentration of RhB. When the concentration was about 150.0mg/L, the removal rate was only 70.0%. The result was attributed to that the MoS2/GQD was few vacant surface sites at higher concentration than 90.0mg/L. Contrarily, the adsorption capacity of the MoS2/GQD increased gradually with the increase of the initial concentration of RhB as shown in Fig.7B. When the RhB concentration was 150.0mg/L, the adsorption capacity reached largest value of 285.0mg/g and was slight change at higher concentration of RhB. The result was also attributed to that the surface sites of MoS2/GQD were completely adsorbed at higher concentration than 150.0mg/L. In addition to this, the Fig.7A and B clearly showed that the adsorption reached an equilibrium state in the first 2s. This result further confirmed that the absorbent based on MoS2/GQD was very suitable for quickly removing RhB from water. In a comparison, the Fig.7C shows the similar curves with Fig.7B. The maximum adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD was also about 285.0mg/g without assistant of ultrasound. These results indicated that the effect of ultrasound on adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD was slight. However, it required a longer time to reach adsorption equilibrium for adsorption RhB of MoS2/GQD without assistant of ultrasound.

To further investigate the ultrafast adsorption other dyes over MoS2/GQD under ultrasound assistance, methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO) were chosen to evaluate the adsorption activity of MoS2/GQD. It clearly showed that the MoS2/GQD also exhibited ultrafast adsorption rate to MO and MB as shown in Fig.8 A and B, respectively. Fig.8C and D shows the adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD for MO and MB with various concentrations under ultrasound, respectively. The maximum adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD was about 140.0mg/g and 210.0mg/g for MO and MB under ultrasound assistance, respectively. These results further indicated that MoS2/GQD could effectively and quickly remove dyes by the adsorption method.

As well-knows the adsorption capacity of adsorbents strongly depended on treatment temperature and pH. So, it leaded to additional cost and time due to adjust the treatment temperature and pH. Here, in order to investigate the interference of temperature and pH on adsorption capacity of MoS2/GQD, a series of interference measurements were carried out as shown in Fig. 9 . When the temperature of RhB solution was improved from 20.0°C to 80.0°C, the adsorption capacity was almost no change as shown in Fig.9A. Similarly, when pH of RhB solution was improved from 2 to 12, the adsorption capacity was also slight change as shown in Fig.9B. The high interference properties were attributed to ultrafast adsorption rate of MoS2/GQD for removing organic dyes. These results further indicated that the organic dyes could be effectively removed from water with minimum interference of treatment pH and temperature, which was very important for its practical application.

The cycling adsorption stability of MoS2/GQD was also very important for practical application in treatment organic dyes of wastewater [25]. The cycling performance of the MoS2/GQD towards removing RhB from water as function of cycles was also characterized as shown in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig.10A, the MoS2/GQD (absorbent 1) was dispersed in RhB aqueous solution and the RhB was removed from the aqueous solution by the adsorption of MoS2/GQD. Furthermore, the MoS2/GQD power adsorbed with RhB (absorbent 2) was dispersed in ethanol solution. The RhB was again desorption from surface of MoS2/GQD in ethanol solution and the MoS2/GQD after desorption could be repeatedly used. The MoS2/GQD exhibited high reclaim ratio of RhB (>98.0%). Furthermore, the adsorption capacity and adsorption rate was slight decrease after three cycling adsorption tests as shown in Fig.10B. Fig.10C shows the XRD patterns of MoS2/GQD before and after three cycles, no obvious difference in the spectrum was observed. The FT-IR spectra of MoS2/GQD after adsorption and desorption with RhB were also characterized and compared as shown in Fig.10D. It clearly presented some new peaks for the MoS2/GQD after adsorption with RhB comparing to IR spectrum of pure MoS2/GQD (in Fig.4B). These new peaks were attributed to the RhB comparing to the IR spectrum of pure RhB as shown in Fig.10D(a). In addition to this, some absorption peaks assigned to RhB were shift and broaden. These results further confirmed that the RhB could be adsorbed on surface of MoS2/GQD by the chemical interaction in aqueous solution. Furthermore, as shown in Fig.10D(c), the MoS2/GQD after desorption with RhB showed similar with IR spectrum of pure MoS2/GQD before absorption RhB and the IR absorption peaks of RhB could not observed. The result further confirmed that the RhB could be desorption from surface of MoS2/GQD in ethanol solution and the structure of MoS2/GQD was not change before and after absorption RhB. These results indicated that the MoS2/GQD possessed good recyclability and stability for removing RB from water, in which the MoS2/GQD could be applied as efficient absorbent of dyes for spill emergency treatment.

In summary, the MoS2/GQD has been have been successfully synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method, which could be used as an efficient, robust and recyclable adsorbent for water treatment application. Furthermore, the adsorbent based on GQD and MoS2 showed ultrafast adsorption rate (2s) under ultrasound assistance and slight dependence of treatment temperature and pH. These results did not only proved the synthesis of adsorbent with high performance, but also provided a new method to design and synthesize adsorbent with high ultrafast adsorption rate.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants 11202006 and 11202007, and the Shanxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2014021018-6).
